core_meaning: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる covers a wide range of 'going up' or 'rising' — physically moving to a higher place, a level or number increasing, or a state improving/completing. It's also used for entering an indoor space (like stepping up into a house from the entryway), nervousness in performance contexts, and as a compound verb suffix meaning 'finished up.'

sentences.jp: <ruby>緊張<rt>きんちょう</rt></ruby>しすぎて、スピーチ<ruby>中<rt>ちゅう</rt></ruby>ずっと<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がってたよ。
sentences.translation: I was so nervous I was totally flustered the whole time during my speech.
sentences.notes: This uses <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる in the sense of 'getting stage fright / freezing up from nerves' — common in spoken Japanese when someone chokes under pressure.

sentences.jp: <ruby>靴<rt>くつ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>脱<rt>ぬ</rt></ruby>いで、どうぞ<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がってください。
sentences.translation: Please take off your shoes and come in.
sentences.notes: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる here means stepping up into a home from the <ruby>玄関<rt>げんかん</rt></ruby> — a culturally specific use tied to the raised floor level of Japanese interiors.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>辺<rt>へん</rt></ruby>、<ruby>最近<rt>さいきん</rt></ruby><ruby>家賃<rt>やちん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がってきてるらしいよ。
sentences.translation: Apparently rent around here has been going up lately.
sentences.notes: Shows the 'price/level rising' sense — <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がってきている signals an ongoing upward trend over time.

sentences.jp: <ruby>気温<rt>きおん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がるにつれて、<ruby>冷房<rt>れいぼう</rt></ruby>の<ruby>需要<rt>じゅよう</rt></ruby>も<ruby>増<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>える。
sentences.translation: As the temperature rises, demand for air conditioning increases as well.
sentences.notes: A natural written/formal collocation pairing <ruby>気温<rt>きおん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる with the につれて construction to describe proportional change.

sentences.jp: レポート、さっき<ruby>書<rt>か</rt></ruby>き<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がったから、あとで<ruby>送<rt>おく</rt></ruby>るね。
sentences.translation: I just finished writing the report, so I'll send it to you later.
sentences.notes: <ruby>書<rt>か</rt></ruby>き<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる is a compound verb meaning 'to finish writing (completely)' — this suffix sense of <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる appears across many verbs like <ruby>出来上<rt>できあ</rt></ruby>がる and <ruby>仕上<rt>しあ</rt></ruby>がる.

dont_use: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる is intransitive — don't use it where you need a transitive verb. If you're raising something yourself, use <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げる instead: say <ruby>音量<rt>おんりょう</rt></ruby>を<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げて (turn up the volume), not ×<ruby>音量<rt>おんりょう</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げる. Also avoid using <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる for abstract 'improvement' when <ruby>上手<rt>うま</rt></ruby>くなる or <ruby>伸<rt>の</rt></ruby>びる would sound more natural — saying <ruby>英語<rt>えいご</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がった for 'my English improved' is understood but less idiomatic than <ruby>英語<rt>えいご</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上手<rt>うま</rt></ruby>くなった.

confused_with.contrast: Learners often want to say <ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がった for 'I climbed the mountain,' but <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる sounds odd there — <ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>に<ruby>上<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>る is the natural choice for climbing something tall like a mountain, stairs, or a tree by your own effort over distance. <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる is better for a short step up (entering a house, getting onto a stage) or for abstract rises (prices, temperature, skill level).

confusion_set.use_when: Something rises on its own (intransitive) — prices, temperature, stepping into a home, finishing up a task, or nerves spiking.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>気温<rt>きおん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がって、アイスが<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べたくなった。

confusion_set.use_when: You actively raise or lift something (transitive) — turning up volume, raising your hand, giving something upward.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>先生<rt>せんせい</rt></ruby>に<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>われて<ruby>手<rt>て</rt></ruby>を<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げた。

confusion_set.use_when: You physically climb something tall or ascend over a distance — mountains, slopes, stairs, or the sun rising in the sky.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>子<rt>こ</rt></ruby>どものころ、よく<ruby>木<rt>き</rt></ruby>に<ruby>上<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>ったな。

frequency: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる is extremely common across all registers — daily conversation, news, and writing. Its many senses (physical, abstract, compound verb) make it one of the highest-frequency motion/change verbs in the language.
anki_hint: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる is always intransitive: things or people rise on their own — if YOU are raising something, switch to <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げる.
